case_name: David Allen, Individually, and as Beneficiary of the Estate of Priscilla W. Allen v. Peter Allen

segments.text: May it please the court, I'm Tom Pappas, and I have the privilege of representing Peter Allen in this case.
The court should reverse and vacate the probate court's order because the probate court lacked subject matter jurisdiction.
David Allen himself lacked standing to bring the claims, and the estate was an indispensable party, and they were not a party below.
If the court reaches the other two issues raised on appeal, the court should also reverse the ruling on attorney's fees and the capacity issue.
Now, the probate court's decision had some 25 pages of facts, which I believe demonstrates it's a highly contestant matter below,
and not litigation of a clearly defined and established right, but only a handful of the facts are necessary for this appeal.
Here, David Allen was a stranger to the deeded issue. He was neither grantor nor grantee.
He brought an equitable action to set aside the 25-15 deed five years later in 2020, and two years after his mother had died.
So knowing he did notify or didn't conclude the trustee as a part of the act?
No, the trustee was named as an interested party, but he wasn't actually a defendant below. Peter Allen was a sole defendant below.
In Priscilla Allen, first and foremost, if David Allen had standing, that means he had to have some interest in the real estate issue,
because if he had no interest, and we don't think he did, but if he had an interest, that means that the court was deciding interests in real estate.
And in order for a court to decide interest in real estate, all parties who have any interest in that real estate need to get parties.
And if the estate was not a party, it had an interest because she owned half the real estate, and therefore they were an indispensable party.
So that's why it's important that they were not.
Do you essentially sign off on the part where they are?
Well, no. The state clearly had half interest in the property because it was included in the property.
Sure, sure.
And the executor of the estate, an independent executor...
If the state was going to have its own interest on that, it wouldn't matter whether the other half...
I don't think it said it didn't care. If you look at the court's order on that matter,
David Allen objected to the inventory on the same grounds here,
and the executor made the decision not to pursue those claims.
So I don't think it was... I don't care. It made the decision as an independent executor.
I'm not pursuing those claims.
If the trustee is named as an interested party, how is that in Adam?
Because an interested party is not a defendant, so they didn't participate in the trial.
Presumably, the executor would have a choice to either be...
If they're named as a defendant, to then defend Priscilla's actions,
because the executor steps in the shoes of Priscilla.
Did they signal that they weren't going to?
Well, they signaled that they didn't have... they weren't going to,
but that's a decision that I think they're entitled to make.
And I think you can take that decision that we don't care,
or we wouldn't defend her actions.
It's in essence, by not including it in the inventory,
it's an indirect way of saying,
we agree that she needed to have interest,
and we're not going to try to bring it back into the estate.
But they also could have intervened for anything.
Well, they could have intervened, but they didn't,
and they weren't named as a party.
They were an indispensable party.
They had an interest in the real estate.
If David Allen had standing, we don't think he did,
but if he had standing, then they needed to be a party
if the court were going to consider all the interest in the real estate,
because you can't have a essentially quiet title action
unless all of the parties who have interest in the real estate
are parties of the action.
So would that have solved the subject manager's addiction?
No, I don't think so.
I don't think they had a direct relationship anyways.
Even if the state was a party.
Correct.
I think the subject manager's addiction goes to whether or not
the claims relate to the estate.
I mean, that's the Rogers case.
Rogers essentially said,
you have to have a direct relationship
between the claims and the estate,
and here they didn't have a direct relationship.
The estate wasn't a party.
It didn't involve the composition of the estate.
That was decided in a separate probate matter.
Well, it did.
But under that analysis,
any heir or will beneficiary could challenge
any intervobal transfer at any time during the decedent's life
to say, look, I have this expectancy interest.
Therefore, I can challenge what the decedent did during their life.
It's the passage of five years.
I think the distinction is,
well, the important part is that
the composition of the estate was decided in a separate probate matter.
Here, it's only an expectancy,
and that's not a sufficient interest.
Not only is it only an expectancy,
but it was not actually against the executor.
It didn't involve an asset of the estate
because the half-interest in Peter was not an asset of the estate.
It didn't involve the administration of the estate.
It didn't involve the probate court's expertise
of examining a will or a trust.
If you look at Rogers,
Rogers was an action against the executor,
in his capacity as executor,
against a property that was in the estate.
They were fighting over a piece of property in the estate,
and it involved settlement of the estate.
And on those facts, this court said that was too indirect
to be grant jurisdiction.
Here, it's not even close to being that direct.
It's not against the executor.
It's not against the executor's capacity executor.
It doesn't involve the property of the estate.
The composition of the estate was settled in a separate matter.
It didn't involve the settlement because that was in a separate matter.
The facts here are much further removed than the Rogers case.
So if Rogers didn't find a direct relationship,
this is even further removed.
Is the fact that he wasn't discovered criminal?
I don't think so.
I don't think so.
Look, the dates were recorded.
It's noticed to the public.
It's noticed to the world.
I don't think that in itself can confer a jurisdiction.
Perhaps not.
Perhaps not.
But I don't think that fact can confer a jurisdiction on the court.
So to challenge that interviolence transfer,
what should have happened?
Either Priscilla herself could have challenged it.
And if you look at the cases cited,
usually it's the party who granted the deed was challenging it.
Or a guardian for Priscilla could have challenged it.
And therefore that is during her life.
After she passed, the executor had the right to challenge it.
He decided not to.
And I think he's the only one that had the right to do so.
Regardless of all that, I think it belonged in superior court.
I don't think the probate court had jurisdiction.
It's not important.
Even if the executor brought the challenge?
Absolutely.
And executors bring check cases in superior court all the time.
Could it have been brought into the court?
No, I don't think so because usually in that case,
they're naming the executors the defendant.
And if you look at the statute,
it says the executor is the defendant.
So I don't, it could have been a DJ,
but I think that it would have been superior court.
I don't think...
Why couldn't the quiet title portion of the probate
jurisdictional statute have provided jurisdiction?
Because that statute says you have to sue the executor
and the asset has to be an asset of the estate
in order to give jurisdiction to the probate court
to quiet title.
So that's a very limited jurisdictional grant
of quiet titles in a probate court.
You have to name the executor,
and the asset has to be an asset of the estate.
Neither of the case here.
If you look at the statute, it says that,
and there's a case from 1975,
which is the Frew v. Dachsh case,
150 NH274, where the court noted
that you got to sue the executor in order to grant...
I mean, I read the statute a little more broadly.
How do you respond to the point that it says
trust assets over which the probate court has jurisdiction?
Well, first of all, this was not a trust asset.
This is an asset outside of probate.
So this wasn't a trust asset,
and that's the first requirement for jurisdiction
for the probate court in a quiet title action.
It has to be an asset of the estate,
and this was not an asset of the estate.
And I think that alone says
the probate court didn't have jurisdiction.
Well, half of it is an asset of the estate.
It seems a little silly that you could litigate
one half in the probate court
and the other half in a different court.
Well, no, because the half being challenged
was not an asset of the estate,
and they're tenants in common,
so they have separate interests,
and that's why it requires to go to spirit court.
But it's your theory that it's not an asset of the estate.
It's the theory on the other side
of what it is an asset of the estate.
Wouldn't that fall within the jurisdiction
of everybody's family?
Well, that would say
you have to win to get jurisdiction,
because if you lost,
it wouldn't have been an asset of the estate.
So you can't sort of bootstrap jurisdiction
by saying, if I win,
therefore it's an asset of the estate,
therefore you had jurisdiction.
Jurisdiction is fundamental, it's at the beginning,
and because at the beginning of this case
of the estate, you can't then say,
well, if I win, it becomes an asset.
I don't think you can bootstrap jurisdiction that way.
Does David's status as a beneficiary
of the estate
weigh into the standing analysis?
No, because I think
he only had an expectancy interest.
He didn't have a legal
or equitable interest.
He wasn't grant or grantee,
so no privity of the state or contract,
no privity of the state,
and most he had an expectancy interest.
But that's only an expectancy
because I'm a beneficiary of a will
and I think that belongs
in the estate.
That's an expectancy interest.
And I think
I don't think...
No, I think it's an important distinction
because it's not standing cases
you have to have either a legal
or equitable interest. He had an expectancy interest.
And I think that's different.
And so I don't think
he had standing because he only had
an expectancy interest. But
if you assume
that he had standing and he had
an interest,
that means that
he hasn't...that gets back to
the indispensable part
of the issue. Because then
you've got David as
plaintiff
saying I got this expectancy interest.
You have the estate that clearly has a legal interest.
It's not party to the case.
And therefore, I think that
that would
make them an indispensable party.
So I think it has both ways.
I don't think he can say I can have standing
but they're not an indispensable party.
But first and foremost, I don't think
he had standing. I don't think
that expectancy interest itself
is sufficient to grant standing.
Because in that case
that would mean that
any fair or
will beneficiary
would challenge any
intervivos transfer at any time
during the scene's life
in probate court. And that would make
the probate court a court of
general equitable jurisdiction.
And frankly, it's not one.

May it please the court, I'm Tom Pappas, and I have the privilege of representing Peter Allen in this case.

The court should reverse and vacate the probate court's order because the probate court lacked subject matter jurisdiction.

David Allen himself lacked standing to bring the claims, and the estate was an indispensable party, and they were not a party below.

If the court reaches the other two issues raised on appeal, the court should also reverse the ruling on attorney's fees and the capacity issue.

Now, the probate court's decision had some 25 pages of facts, which I believe demonstrates it's a highly contestant matter below,

and not litigation of a clearly defined and established right, but only a handful of the facts are necessary for this appeal.

Here, David Allen was a stranger to the deeded issue. He was neither grantor nor grantee.

He brought an equitable action to set aside the 25-15 deed five years later in 2020, and two years after his mother had died.

So knowing he did notify or didn't conclude the trustee as a part of the act?

No, the trustee was named as an interested party, but he wasn't actually a defendant below. Peter Allen was a sole defendant below.

In Priscilla Allen, first and foremost, if David Allen had standing, that means he had to have some interest in the real estate issue,

because if he had no interest, and we don't think he did, but if he had an interest, that means that the court was deciding interests in real estate.

And in order for a court to decide interest in real estate, all parties who have any interest in that real estate need to get parties.

And if the estate was not a party, it had an interest because she owned half the real estate, and therefore they were an indispensable party.

So that's why it's important that they were not.

Do you essentially sign off on the part where they are?

Well, no. The state clearly had half interest in the property because it was included in the property.

Sure, sure.

And the executor of the estate, an independent executor...

If the state was going to have its own interest on that, it wouldn't matter whether the other half...

I don't think it said it didn't care. If you look at the court's order on that matter,

David Allen objected to the inventory on the same grounds here,

and the executor made the decision not to pursue those claims.

So I don't think it was... I don't care. It made the decision as an independent executor.

I'm not pursuing those claims.

If the trustee is named as an interested party, how is that in Adam?

Because an interested party is not a defendant, so they didn't participate in the trial.

Presumably, the executor would have a choice to either be...

If they're named as a defendant, to then defend Priscilla's actions,

because the executor steps in the shoes of Priscilla.

Did they signal that they weren't going to?

Well, they signaled that they didn't have... they weren't going to,

but that's a decision that I think they're entitled to make.

And I think you can take that decision that we don't care,

or we wouldn't defend her actions.

It's in essence, by not including it in the inventory,

it's an indirect way of saying,

we agree that she needed to have interest,

and we're not going to try to bring it back into the estate.

But they also could have intervened for anything.

Well, they could have intervened, but they didn't,

and they weren't named as a party.

if the court were going to consider all the interest in the real estate,

because you can't have a essentially quiet title action

unless all of the parties who have interest in the real estate

are parties of the action.

So would that have solved the subject manager's addiction?

No, I don't think so.

I don't think they had a direct relationship anyways.

Even if the state was a party.

Correct.

I think the subject manager's addiction goes to whether or not

the claims relate to the estate.

I mean, that's the Rogers case.

Rogers essentially said,

you have to have a direct relationship

between the claims and the estate,

and here they didn't have a direct relationship.

The estate wasn't a party.

It didn't involve the composition of the estate.

That was decided in a separate probate matter.

Well, it did.

But under that analysis,

any heir or will beneficiary could challenge

any intervobal transfer at any time during the decedent's life

to say, look, I have this expectancy interest.

I think the distinction is,
